I have worked in IT for ten years.

We have just finished the new version.
I have never failed, and I learned a lot when I started.
Hi Daniela, good to see you again. I have just moved to this office last month. Have you worked here long?
Welcome! Yes, I have worked here for eight years. I started in 2017 as an analyst.
Eight years, impressive. Have you ever managed an international experience like a global project?
Yes, I have. Last year I led a project with the Lisbon team, and we finished it in December.
That is great. I have never worked with that team, but I have heard they are excellent.
They are. We have already started a new project together, and we have not finished it yet.
So you have done a lot recently. When did you become a manager?
I became a manager three years ago. Since then, I have grown so much.
I have worked here for eight years.
Have you ever led a big project?
I finished the report yesterday.
I have not finished it yet.
We have just launched the new system.

Hi Daniela, it is Sarah, your English coach. Great rhythm last time. Today, a quick story about my career, so you can hear two tenses working together. I have worked in technology for fifteen years. I started as a junior analyst in 2009, in a small company. That job taught me everything. Since then, I have changed companies three times, and I have learned something new in each one. Two years ago, I moved to my current team, and I have been very happy here. Notice the pattern. When I give a finished time, like in 2009 or two years ago, I use the past simple: I started, I moved. When I talk about my life experience, with no exact time, I use the present perfect: I have worked, I have learned. Have you ever thought about your own story like this? Try it. Talk soon.

Hey Daniela, it is Bruno from the Lisbon office. I want to tell you about a project, because it is a good example of these two tenses. Last year, our team built a new payment system. We finished it in November, and we launched it in December. Those are finished actions, with a clear time, so I use the past simple: we built, we finished, we launched. But the project is not over for me. I have learned so much from it, and I have already started a new version. I have not stopped improving it yet. Do you see the difference? The finished events are past simple. The experience and the recent results, with no exact time, are present perfect. By the way, congratulations on your progress. You have come a long way. See you at the meeting.
